samples.title: How Chopin Approaches a Harmonic Climax / Chromatic Mediants / Ger6+
samples.rationale: Classical harmonic analysis with narrated Chopin examples (chromatic mediants and augmented-sixth harmony).

speechSegments.text: I think it's obvious that sometimes a composer just wants to overcharge a listener and I would say that this kind of hysterical breakdowns make an indispensable feature of chapon's Music in general so I want to talk about how such a climax is being constructed through certain specific devices like chromatic medians and rather flamboyant executions of German six chords All In All romantic cliches that I'm going to dive in a little deeper so see you back in a few seconds

are your commercials they suck ass she better act in an epileptic whouse is that like the one your mom works at maybe it's good to know what a chromatic median relation actually is because this plays a certain role in this video Let's Take F major as key center with the Constitutional fifths relations above and below in the middle of two chords that are related by the fifth lies the median and that's exactly what the term median means in the middle these are called diatonic medians because they can be generated from the same home scale and those always have two tones in common and modulating into one of those is a no-brainer as you can just use a secondary dominant and confirm afterwards

with the chromatic medians though it's a little more complex if you manipulate a diatonic framework by adding accidentals it becomes chromatic for example as major chords related by a minor third or a major third and those cord relations sound a little more flamboyant as there is no diatonic connection anymore and the contrast of color when you connect those chords is much brighter than in the diatonic relations and you really can feel feel the
distance you got the point and I guess we're finally prepared for the show pen

so let's try to understand what's actually happening the passage leading into the actual Outburst establishes five one pendulums above ascending baselines and whilst this pendulum is cooking Chapan is increasing the tension on two other parameters gradually before suddenly erupting into a great major key Cadence plus doubling down on a chromatic median right away it makes a lot of sense to categorize the actual climax as a succession of two separate cadences the first one related to E major and the second one surprisingly to a flat major more accurately G sharp major as the more convenient notation in a flat conceals the actual median relation between those keys now the legitimate harmonic connection between those two cadences takes place within the fraction of a second as chopan smuggles in the D natural on the last speat of the E major bar which turns it into an E7 but that's being resolved as a German sixth chord instantly and that's actually quite a lot of harmonic information to digest for such a brief moment when you take into account that already the E major Cadence pretty much came as a sudden surprise bending a major Triad into a German sixth chord and resolve it into a cadential 64 that lies a semmit tone below is actually an easy pattern to memorize for a chromatic

modulation and this modulation can actually be practiced in Cycles when you repeat the procedure two times the same way it'll lead you back into the key you started from and that's called a major third cycle in which the roots of each of the participating Keys form an augmented Triad in other words the three cores on top here make up a little family of chromatic medians needless to say and it was actually These Bars that inspired the following little improv

maybe let's take the occasion and check out some more flamboyant stuff on the German six chord and you know I like to share my own experiments the German six chord makes especially a good deal breaker to dissolve a chromatic field or sequence with a triumphant gesture and in the following example I'm trying to intensify the situation by using the German sixth cord modulation twice within a relatively short time span to create harmonic overcharge

if you want to create this kind of effect it's important to know that it is always the cadential 64 that locks in the new key and which usually is the Pinnacle and actual Target of the climax I admit that this one is a rather synthetical example as the harmonic plan is kind of random so I decided to set myself a more demanding compositional task by composing a coda to a hypothetical piece in D Major and you probably know that the codas are this kind of situations where you pull the more special devices out of the trunk let's listen

I'm trying to give you a quick rundown I began the coder by settling D major as home key on a pedal point with a dominant tonic pendulum on top of it then all of a sudden I just throw in a chord that anyone listening probably would perceive as dominant seventh chord but as you can see on the paper it is a German sixth chord related to a minor there is no real functional connection here but broken down into the sheer chords you can see that this core progression is actually the result of a voice leading procedure that can be described as chromatic contrary motion of the outer voices this minor 64 chord marks the beginning of a real sequence of alternating minor 64 chords in a diminished seventh chord A semitone below when chained up like this you obtain a baseline that descends by semmit tones the actual climax of this coder takes place when that sequence runs into a 51 pendulum in E Flat and I guess here at latest The Listener lost harmonic orientation due to the preceding sequence and confirming a distant key like E flat by a dominant pendulum seems kind of off but there is a quick way out because the dominant of E flat can as well be used as the German six chord in the context of D
major and that's what I'm going to do so when it swings to the dominant the third time I bended over into the credential 64 of D major and the party is over maybe let's give it another listen

samples.title: Learn the piano chords to ‘Purple Rain’ by Prince -- Playground Sessions
samples.rationale: Short pop tutorial combining left-hand chords, right-hand melody, and arrangement ideas for Purple Rain.

speechSegments.text: what's up everybody theron brown here and welcome to playground sessions youtube channel today is a special day we are celebrating prince's birthday june 7th so right now we're going to look at one of his most popular songs purple rain which this song is also available in the app at all levels but right now i'm going to give you a little overview of the song we're going to go over the chords and the melody and it's a lot easier than you think so first we're going to deal with some triads let's check it out okay so this song uses four chords which these chords you're going to see in this progression that they're using a lot of different songs so we're in the key of b flat so you'll see the one chord which is b flat you also see the four chord e flat the five chord which is f and even the sixth chord which is g
let's play those real quick together again b flat and e flat

and then g remember we're playing all of these in root position now this song actually has a break in it that leads into the chorus it's going to start on our five chord which is f okay let's take a look at that now we're going to hold this chord for four beats one measure one two three four and a b-flat that's when our course is going to come in here's what sounds like with the melody over it
next you would hear the famous line
now here we're going to go to our four chord which is e flat
now we're back to our one chord just b flat we're gonna have a little passing and go to g minor
now we're on our five chord which is f we're going to stay there and resolve to our b flat okay so now we're going to put this into context remember we're going to start on your five chord three four
to your one chord now to your four chord three four
two one chord two now to your six five chord

one chord all right so that's the basic structure of the song using the chords and the progression now remember that little part at the end you're going to want to count through because it goes on for a little a little bit of a long time but it's all good if you count you'll land in the right spot now we're going to check out some advancements that i like to do personally have some fun on this song and i i suggest that you try it too all right let's check it out okay so remember you can make this song your own just like i'm getting ready to do you might hear some octaves or even some fifths in the bass there to give it some extra um and you also might hear some grace notes in my right hand that always sounds like what i like to call saucy if you will all right let's try it out

all right good people thanks for watching i'm theron brown don't forget to hit subscribe and tap that notification bell be sure to check out the playground sessions at co-created by music legend quincy jones playground teaches the piano with interactive feedback and gaming features all while using your favorite songs peace out

samples.title: Clearing Up Confusion About All Of The Ornaments in the Bach Minuet in G BWV Anh 114 (Petzold)
samples.rationale: Baroque ornamentation lesson covering mordents, appoggiaturas, and trills in the Minuet in G.

speechSegments.text: hello and thank you for watching I'm Jennifer Bowman with Olympia piano and in this short video I'm going to go over all the ornamentation that is written in box minuette and G this minuette and actually this minuette now is not attributed to box but another composer named Christian petel but regardless let's go through the ornaments that were actually written in the score so what I'm going to do is go through chronologically in the music so we're going to start with measure three you'll see in measure three there the ornament it looks like a little squiggle with a line through it this ornament is called a Morant and what it means is to play the main note then the note underneath and then back to the main note so it's a three note ornament and if you look above I've written out how you're supposed to play it in the Baroque Era which is starting on the beat so I'd like to offer you two fingerings for how to play this and then I want to show you one other thing before moving on to the next one either way we're going to start on finger three for the E so you can either cross over with 1 2 1 for the Morant or you can do 1 2 3 so I'll show you both of those so here's the one two one first we have three 1 2 1 or 3 1 2 3 so one of those may work for you or you can experiment with other fingering to see what best fits your hand the other thing I want to point out is traditionally in the Baroque Period which is the time period of which this piece was written this ornament was played on the beat however I wanted to just show you that even though that's the way you're supposed to play it you will often hear it before the beat which would sound like this so now I'm going to play all of those with all the different fingerings so here's the traditional way finger one two one with the left hand so one 2 3 1 2 3 1 2 3 now here's the alternate fingering 1 2 3 1 2 2 3 here's how you'll sometimes hear it different interpretation of the ornaments see I played that before and so forth so here's the ornament in measure five I like to use fingers 32 three for this one you can see what might work for your hand but here it is on the beat which would be the Baroque correct way of playing it so it would sound like this it would be the first note of the Morant would be played with the left hand however you will also hear it with the Morant being played kind of like Grace notes which would mean that you'd Play 3 2 and then the second C would come with the left hand so you might hear slight subtle difference moving on in measure eight we have what's called an apur and what this is is it looks like a Grace note and we've got to just givey up the counting a little bit differently than it's written it's not going to come before the beat in this case I'll show you two different ways this is commonly played the most commonly accepted way of playing this apura is giving the B One Beat and giving the a two beats so that would add up to three beats so it would sound like this with the left hand 1 and 2 and 3 and however you will also hear it interpreted as an eighth note and an eighth note with a tie like this 1 and 2 and 3 and but the first way is the more correct way of playing it then moving forward Measure 11 we have the same as Measure 3 measure 13 we have the same as measure five so our final ornament written out in this piece is a Trill which happens in measure 30 it's a little squiggly line no slash through it this time so that means this one is going to be four or more notes just depends on your mood and how you'd like to play this one the written note is B but the symbol means to start on the Note above so we're going to play Four notes and we're going to start on a c the note next door to it if you look above I'm going to give you two options for the trill the one I usually like to play is a written out four note Trill just like four 16th notes the only tricky thing is starting on beat one with finger four we're going to switch to finger three to play the trill because finger three is a stronger finger so it's going to look like this if you'd like to be a little bit more elaborate you can add two more notes to the trill and have it be a little bit more like a triplet in flavor here it is with four with six so choose which one you like better now those are the only ornaments that are written out in the original edition of this piece however you should also know that ornamentation in the Baroque Period was also a form of improvization so often people would just make up ornaments as they went along so I'm going to play the first section adding some ornaments that might have been done during this time period I'll play The Originals as well here we go

so if you're practicing this piece and you want to have a little fun try out some different ornaments but the other thing I just wanted to mention before the end of this video is usually when I'm adding the ornaments in this piece I usually play the first time through Straight and the second time with the ornaments I hope this video helped you understand the ornamentations in this minuette and g a little bit better I also want to mention I have a companion video which explores some of the articulations you can use when playing this piece so be sure to check that out as always I hope you'll subscribe to my channel for more videos on piano technique piano literature and quick piano tips like this video thanks for

samples.title: Stride Blues Piano Bass Lesson with Jonny May
samples.rationale: Compact stride-blues bass lesson with spoken instruction and keyboard examples.

speechSegments.text: Hey there, I'm Johnny May, music notes teacher and Disneyland pianist. And today I'm going to teach you a stride blues pattern for intermediate and advanced players. Now a stride blues is where the left hand jumps from root to chord and it creates this really full and energetic sound. So if you want to learn this technique, let's get started. So here's what it sounds like. I'll play it for you and then we'll break it down. Here it is.

G7 four. Okay, great job. So this is kind of a lazy blues pattern. So first looking at the quarter note pattern, basically the way this left hand pattern works is you take the chord, you know, in this case it's a C7. And so the pattern goes like this. You go root of the chord, which is C, and I use this inversion of a C7. Okay? Now this is E, G, B flat, and C. Okay? Now, when we play chords in the stride pattern, we generally like to stay right in the middle of the piano. And my range, I think that sounds the best, is right between this C and about this A. I don't like to go much higher than this and lower than this. Okay? Because it's emulating a guitar, right? Boom chick. Boom chick. Right? And this is kind of a mid-range. A guitar is kind of a mid-range instrument. Okay? So, chords work in this general area. Okay? So, I'm going to go root chord and then I go to the fifth of the chord. And the fifth of C is G. And then back up to the chord. Okay? And that's the basic pattern. Root to chord, fifth to chord. Okay? We do this for all the chords. So for F7, there's the root and then the chord is kind of a lower chord here. This is C, E flat, F, and A. Okay? So kind of memorize this position for your F7. And once again, the pattern here will be F chord and then the fifth of F is actually a C and back to the chord. Okay, so you can practice each of these patterns. You know, you can kind of just loop each one and get really comfortable with it. Okay, the G7 is root here and the chords here. This is D, F, G, and B. Okay, so that's root chord. Fifth is D chord. Okay. So, let's go ahead and play this in the 12 bar blues form. Okay. So, here's the first one. One. So, that's measure one. Okay. Now, for measure two, we'll go to our F7. Okay. There's our F. 2 3 4. Then measure three and four we'll do our C7. 1 2 3 4 to 1 2 3 4. Okay. Next measure is an F7. actually rather two measures. So we have 1 2 3 4 1 2 3 4 back to our C7. 2 3 4 2 3 4 Okay, here's measure nine of the form. This is our G7. 1 2 3 4 to our F7. 1 2 3 4 and our C7. 1 2 3 4 and then the quick G7. 1 2 3 4 at the end. Okay, let's go ahead and add that fun little pattern on the right hand. You know, 1 and 2 and 3 and four. Okay, we'll go slowly so you can see how they line up. Okay, here it is. 1 and 2 and 3 and 4 and 1 and 2 and 3 and four and 1 and 2 and 3 and 4 and 1 and 2 and 3 and 4. F7. 1 and 2 and 3 and 4 and 1 and 2 and 3 and 4 and 1 and 2 and 3 and four and 1 and 2 and 3. Here's our G7. L. 1 and 2 and 3 and 1 and 2 and 3 and 4 and 1 and 2 and 3 and four and our G7. 1 and 2 and 3 and four and. Hey, thanks for watching. And if you enjoyed this lesson, you can find more of my lessons at piano.com. And right now, we're giving away a free background track to this lesson. You can get that at piano.com. Well, I really enjoyed this. I hope to see you next time. And thanks for watching.

samples.title: How to use the Sustaining Pedal on Piano!
samples.rationale: Technique lesson focused on sustaining-pedal use with audible demonstrations.

speechSegments.text: hello this is Agnes with piano talent TV in today's video we're going to talk about four different uses of sustaining pedal there are many ways of how to use pedal there is half barrel quarter pedal full panel vibrato panel harmonica pedal and could help to change colors and achieve all kind of different results this is the most common way of peddling a lot of my students hojae's begin to use peddle are often pedaling at the same time as playing the

and what it gives it gives for the phrase and the music to be cut in little pieces and therefore it doesn't sound like it's one United beautiful piece of music what I'm suggesting is pedaling after the notes are being played so I guess the best way to do that would be to say that your foot should be behind your hands a split second behind so we'll just give you a little example of what I mean in slow motion first chord is C major press pedal down and press the C major chord now relieve the hands keep the pedal down and then press the next chord you will hear two different harmonics many harmonies managing now relieve the pedal and put a belt down again this will help you to keep the line yet we'll clean the harmony for you and back to the next chord press the cord it's fellow relieve their hands press the chord change pedal release the hands so your foot is always a little bit late now when I was doing this exercise I was obviously doing everything slow motion when you were performing your piece of music you will be required to move your foot as much quicker right so let's try I'll show you in shop and often up was 37 number one

a lot of the times the accompaniment in musical consists of a bass note plus a court played in the left hand often the bass note and a chord will be a part of the same key signature for example in the bank number for F minor by Frederic
or in most of East nocturnes in this point a case I'm looking at office 37 number one

often students will peddle these two or three cords separately splitting the music into little pieces this kind of pedaling really creates little holes and cuts the piece so we find it difficult to follow the phrasing instead of pedaling separately I would suggest check whether the bass and the court are from the same key this worth peddling would mainly be used to add echo effect or reduce the echo in between the phrases the movements or during the rests the echo effect is mostly used in concert hall theatres or outside concerts were the acoustic is really dry and there is no resonant the length of the echo effect will depend on how quickly you will release the pedal so for example in the hydrants sonata in eva major 2nd movement

or the last echo effect or without the effect or you barely use pedal toward the end of the passage this is a challenging way to pedal as it requires few things a sensitive ear very quick foot movement and ability to play different pressures to different fingers at the same time in pieces like Rachmaninoff Prelude in C sharp minor this way of pedaling will really open different worlds of color a lot of the times students amateur players even professionals don't always consider different ways of pedaling often playing with full pedal down and not always thinking of the balance between the

you probably heard that the sound appeared to be somewhat muddy and not always clean that is mainly because my foot was fully down but also because there was a lack of balance in between my hands if you press the pedal fully down you might be getting thicker sound and the sound will become much louder to compare it with a quarter pedal down the sound is much thinner and therefore it's much easier to control it try to find ways of applying different pressure to each hand and finger for example giving the mouse power to the top finger of the right hand while playing everything else very lightly Porter battle down pressure on the fifth finger of the right hand and everything else very lightly this way we have different layers of sounds and we can hear each note separately

this way of peddling will allow you to pedal through multiple chords and harmonies opening different spectrum of color and layers of the music without making the sound too muddy that's it for today I hope you enjoyed this video and found it useful please leave your comments in the comment section below and let me know what sort of videos you wish to see in the future as I'm really keen to share my knowledge with you I'll leave you the links to my Instagram Twitter Facebook and website pages in the description below don't forget to subscribe and practice as well

samples.title: Analyzing small works from Goyescas 3. Jácara, the Copla of Love & Death and the Fate motive.
samples.rationale: Classical-form and motive analysis of short works from Granados's Goyescas.

speechSegments.text: Hi, I'm Alex Wasil. In this video, I will talk about another small composition by Granados called Hakara. This piece belongs to the Goesque musical world of Granados, but does not belong to the popular suite called Los Mosados. This is one of the tracks included on my new CD of Goes. Here I will talk a little bit about the piece and leave you with my own performance of it at the end of the video. The hakara was a musical form for solo boys with guitar accompaniment that was very popular in Madrid during the time of Goya. This hakala by Granados is subtitled dance for singing and dancing. It is a small work with different parts. The first one imitates the characteristic

The second represents the accompanied boys in a free and robato style. We can also find arpeggios that resemble the characteristic Spanish razados or arpeggios on the guitar. Some sources claim that this composition was created previous to the composition of the sweet Los Mosa Morados. So Granados would have used material from this composition and used it in the sweep. More specifically the theme of the copla, hence its relationship with

A copa is a Spanish popular fclloric song. It is a musical genre of Spain that combines music and words in goes. This is a melody, a theme that we can find several times and represents a sort of painful love song. It appears in different forms in a single melodic line or highly ornamented accompanied or by itself depending always on the different moments of the

This copla theme is indeed a loving climax. But we know that in Granados goes and in fact in some Spanish literature love is closely associated with death. So this theme has a deeper meaning that I will show. As I mentioned before, this copla is not precisely a joyful or happy melody but a painful one given the tragic story of goes. To create that painful feeling, Granados composed it around the theme of the diay or diazer which represents

The DZ is a Gregorian chant, a piece of music that has been widely used throughout the history of music. It describes the last judgment or the day of the wrath and it is used by many composers to describe death. Death is always present in goes. Granados even composed a variation of Diazer in the last piece of the suite representing the spirit of the death
Since love and death are one in the spirit of this music, Granados combined both of them in this theme, ornamenting the death theme, representing the always present death within the love.
Therefore, I personally call this theme the copa of love and death.

and still another idea about this theme that personally helps me to give another dimension to this theme when performing it. If you have watched my video in which I explain about the vagarian nature of some motifs in goes, you'll see how something similar occurs here. The last part of this love and death copla has a three note descending motif that is also recurring in goes. The same way the theme of death or the theme of the laments from Tristan's Bagner can be suggested in some parts of goes as I explained in a previous video. The fate or destiny motive from Bagner's Valkyrie has a similar gesture. The influence and inspiration from Bagner is again present in this theme that can be found in many examples in Goyescas introducing the fate motive in the music making it part of the tragic destiny of love into
Therefore, if we add this fade motif to the previous melody of love and death, this theme to me suggests that in the world of Goescas, The only fate of love is death.

Hakara is indeed a small composition but it contains this theme this copla of love and death that personally knowing all these details helps me to make it more expressive when performing it. I will leave you now with my performance of this piece and I hope you like it.
